hooks.prompt: A Bash tool call just ran; its input is in $ARGUMENTS. FIRST check the executed command string: if it does not contain an actual `git commit` invocation (e.g. it is only git init/add/status/diff or unrelated), or the commit visibly failed, the verification PASSES immediately — do not inspect anything. Otherwise a commit was just created: inspect it with `git show HEAD` (run it in the repository the command ran in — cd there first if the command did). Examine ONLY the added lines (starting with +) for source-code comments that must not be committed: (1) comments explaining WHAT the code does when the code already says it (e.g. '// increment counter', '// loop over items'), (2) comments justifying or narrating the change itself (e.g. '// now handles null correctly', '// changed to use X', '// fixed per review'), (3) AI-assistant-style commentary addressed at a reviewer. These belong in the commit message, not the code. ACCEPTABLE and to be ignored: WHY-comments stating a non-obvious constraint, invariant or workaround (ideally with a ticket reference), license headers, and doc comments (/// XML docs, docstrings) that document a public API contract. If no violating comments were added, the verification passes. If violating comments exist, FAIL the verification and report each one with file path, approximate line, and the comment text, and instruct: remove these comments (moving any genuinely valuable rationale into the commit message) and amend the commit with `git commit --amend`.
